topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s thick parchment felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I stared at the deed, the elegant script mocking me with its formality. Grandma’s house. A sprawling, dusty monument to a life I barely registered. My brother, Mark, practically bounced with excitement, his grin wide and unearned. I just leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest, and let my gaze drift over the peeling wallpaper. It was all so faded, so utterly unremarkable. "Well, isn't this thrilling?" I drawled, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth. I didn't bother to look at Mark. I was already calculating how quickly I could liquidate the assets and disappear before anyone got involved in this mausoleum. The air in the lawyer's office suddenly felt too thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I stood in the foyer, the silence of the old house pressing in on me like physical weight. Every shadow seemed to stretch and twist, making the ornate furniture look grotesque. Mark was already fussing with a dusty antique lamp, humming some irritatingly cheerful tune. I watched him, arms crossed tight over my chest, a faint tightening in my jaw. This entire inheritance felt like an imposition, a tedious obligation thrust upon me by some distant, forgotten relative. "It's certainly… grand," I said, the word tasting like ash. I didn't move to help with the boxes. Instead, I walked toward the grand staircase, my steps measured and deliberate, each one a declaration of my utter disinterest in whatever history this place held. I needed a drink. Something sharp.

[story 3]

The attic air was stale and heavy, smelling of mildew and forgotten lavender. I surveyed the relics of Grandma’s existence—a collection of brittle photographs and moth-eaten textiles—with a profound sense of detachment. Mark was down downstairs, loudly discussing renovations, oblivious to the atmosphere I cultivated. I picked up a tarnished silver locket, turning it over slowly. It was worthless, just old metal. A small, almost imperceptible curl of my lip formed as I examined it. "What's the point of all this?" I muttered to the empty space, my tone laced with a weary finality. I dropped the locket back onto the trunk with a soft, dismissive clink. This whole affair was a waste of time, a tiresome duty I was now forced to endure.
